subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 16! Time is one of the most useful ideas in all of mathematics, because you use it every single day. When Amina works out how long it is until Maghrib prayer, when Yusuf checks whether he can catch the early bus from Hargeisa to Nairobi, or when Khadija plans how many episodes she and her little brother can watch before bedtime, they are all doing time mathematics. In this unit you will become confident and quick with reading, calculating and comparing time in many different forms.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 5 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why does this matter so much? Time keeps the whole world in order. Traders open their stalls at set hours, buses leave on the minute, and the five daily prayers – Fajr at dawn, Dhuhr at midday, Asr in the afternoon, Maghrib at sunset and Isha at night – follow the movement of the sun through the day. When you become confident with time, you become a person who is organised, punctual and dependable. These are skills you will use for the rest of your life, at school, at work and at home.

As you work, keep a pencil and paper beside you, and if you can, a clock or watch too. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Draw the timelines, write the numbers, and say the answers out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Let us begin.
Read and write time in both 12-hour (am/pm) and 24-hour formats, and convert between them.
Convert between units of time: seconds, minutes, hours, days, weeks, months and years.
Calculate the duration between two times, including across an hour boundary and across midnight.
Add and subtract time durations accurately, regrouping in 60s rather than 100s.
Read and interpret timetables, and work out journey times and waiting times.
Explain what time zones are and calculate the time in another city using UTC offsets.
Compare and order times given in different units.

concepts.title: Units of Time and How They Connect
concepts.explanation: Here are the key relationships you must know by heart: 365 days = 1 year (and 366 days in a leap year)
concepts.example: Because time uses 60 and not 100, you can never simply write minutes after a decimal point as if they were part of a hundred. Half an hour is 30 minutes, not 50, because 30 is half of 60. A quarter of an hour is 15…

concepts.title: Converting Between Units of Time
concepts.explanation: To change from a larger unit to a smaller unit, you multiply. To change from a smaller unit to a larger unit, you divide. This is because there are many small units inside each large one. Let us convert 2 hours 15 minutes into minutes. First change the hours: 2 hours = 2 × 60 = 120 minutes. Then add the extra 15 minutes: 120 + 15 = 135 minutes. So 2 hours 15 minutes = 135 minutes.
concepts.example: Now let us go the other way and convert 200 seconds into minutes and seconds. We divide by 60 because there are 60 seconds in a minute: 200 ÷ 60 = 3 remainder 20. The whole part, 3, is the number of minutes; the…

concepts.title: The 12-Hour and 24-Hour Clock
concepts.explanation: There are two common ways to write the time of day, and a confident mathematician can use both. The 12-hour clock counts the hours from 1 to 12 twice each day, and adds am for the morning (from midnight up to noon) and pm for the afternoon and evening (from noon up to midnight). The 24-hour clock counts the hours straight through from 00:00 at midnight to 23:59 just before the next midnight, so it never needs am or pm. The 24-hour clock is used on bus and train timetables, on flight boards at the airport, and in…
concepts.example: Look carefully at the two tricky rows. Midnight is 00:00 in the 24-hour clock, even though we call it 12 o'clock at night. Noon is 12:00, and it stays 12 – we do not add 12 to make 24:00. From 1 pm onwards we add 12: 1…

concepts.title: Calculating Durations (Elapsed Time)
concepts.explanation: A duration is the amount of time between a start time and an end time. Working out durations is one of the most useful time skills of all, and the safest way to do it is the counting-on method using a timeline. Instead of trying to subtract the times directly, which often goes wrong because time is in 60s, we hop forward in easy steps. Let us find the duration from 5:37 pm to 7:15 pm. Draw a simple timeline and hop:
concepts.example: Hop up to the next whole hour. From 5:37 to 6:00 is 23 minutes (because 60 − 37 = 23).

concepts.title: Reading and Using Timetables
concepts.explanation: A timetable is a chart that shows when things happen. Bus and train timetables, school lesson plans, flight boards and TV guides are all timetables. They are usually written in the 24-hour clock, and they are read by matching a row (usually a place or event) with a column (usually a particular bus, train or lesson). The skill is to find exactly the box you need, and then to calculate durations between boxes. Here is a small bus timetable for a route between towns near Nairobi. Each column is one bus service.
concepts.example: Let us answer some questions from this timetable, reading carefully across rows and down columns.

concepts.title: Understanding Time Zones
concepts.explanation: As the Earth spins, the Sun rises on different parts of the world at different moments. When it is bright midday in Mogadishu, it is still the dark middle of the night in New York. So that everyone's clocks match their own daylight, the world is divided into 24 time zones, roughly one for each hour of the day. All the zones are measured from a single starting line that passes through Greenwich in England, called UTC (Coordinated Universal Time). A place to the east of Greenwich is ahead – its clock shows a later…
concepts.example: To find the time in another city, work out the difference between the two zones, then add if you are travelling east (ahead) or subtract if you are travelling west (behind). Let us try one. It is 3:00 pm in London…

methods.title: How to find a duration (hopping method)
steps: Hop from the start time up to the next whole hour.
Hop through the whole hours in between.
Hop the final minutes to the end time.
Add all the hops together, regrouping 60 minutes as 1 hour.

methods.title: How to add or subtract durations
methods.example: Convert 150 minutes into hours and minutes.
steps: Add or subtract the hours, then the minutes, keeping them separate.
If the minutes reach 60 or more, exchange 60 minutes for 1 hour and carry it.
If you cannot subtract the minutes, exchange 1 hour for 60 minutes first.

methods.title: How to find the time in another zone
methods.example: Write 7:45 pm in 24-hour time, and write 06:20 in 12-hour time.
steps: Find the difference in hours between the two UTC offsets.
Add if the other city is east (ahead), subtract if it is west (behind).
Adjust the date if you cross midnight.

methods.title: Duration with the hopping method
methods.example: How long is it from 2:30 pm to 5:15 pm?
steps: Solution: Hop 2:30 to 3:00 is 30 minutes; 3:00 to 5:00 is 2 hours; 5:00 to 5:15 is 15 minutes.
Add: 30 + 15 = 45 minutes, plus 2 hours = 2 hours 45 minutes.
Check the duration with the hopping method result carefully.

methods.title: Duration across midnight
methods.example: How long is it from 10:50 pm to 1:25 am?
steps: Solution: Hop 10:50 pm to 11:00 pm is 10 minutes; 11:00 pm to midnight is 1 hour; midnight to 1:00 am is 1 hour; 1:00 am to 1:25 am is 25 minutes.
Add: 10 + 25 = 35 minutes, plus 2 hours = 2 hours 35 minutes.
Check the duration across midnight result carefully.

methods.title: Adding durations with regrouping
methods.example: Add 2 hours 45 minutes and 1 hour 30 minutes.
steps: Solution: Hours: 2 + 1 = 3.
Minutes: 45 + 30 = 75.
Regroup 75 minutes as 1 hour 15 minutes.
Carry the hour: 3 + 1 = 4 hours, with 15 minutes left, so the answer is 4 hours 15 minutes.

workedExamples.title: Convert to a single unit
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Change the hours first: 3 × 60 = 180 minutes. Then add the extra minutes: 180 + 40 = 220 minutes. (Check: 220 ÷ 60 = 3 remainder 40, which brings us back to 3 hours 40 minutes.)

workedExamples.title: Convert to mixed units
workedExamples.prompt: Convert 150 minutes into hours and minutes.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Divide by 60: 150 ÷ 60 = 2 remainder 30. The whole part is the hours and the remainder is the minutes, so 150 minutes = 2 hours 30 minutes.

workedExamples.title: 12-hour to 24-hour
workedExamples.prompt: Write 7:45 pm in 24-hour time, and write 06:20 in 12-hour time.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: For 7:45 pm, add 12 to the hour: 7 + 12 = 19, so it is 19:45. For 06:20, the hour is under 12 so it stays the same and is morning: 6:20 am.

workedExamples.title: Duration with the hopping method
workedExamples.prompt: How long is it from 2:30 pm to 5:15 pm?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Hop 2:30 to 3:00 is 30 minutes; 3:00 to 5:00 is 2 hours; 5:00 to 5:15 is 15 minutes. Add: 30 + 15 = 45 minutes, plus 2 hours = 2 hours 45 minutes.

workedExamples.title: Duration across midnight
workedExamples.prompt: How long is it from 10:50 pm to 1:25 am?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Hop 10:50 pm to 11:00 pm is 10 minutes; 11:00 pm to midnight is 1 hour; midnight to 1:00 am is 1 hour; 1:00 am to 1:25 am is 25 minutes. Add: 10 + 25 = 35 minutes, plus 2 hours = 2 hours 35 minutes.

workedExamples.title: Adding durations with regrouping
workedExamples.prompt: Add 2 hours 45 minutes and 1 hour 30 minutes.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Hours: 2 + 1 = 3. Minutes: 45 + 30 = 75. Regroup 75 minutes as 1 hour 15 minutes. Carry the hour: 3 + 1 = 4 hours, with 15 minutes left, so the answer is 4 hours 15 minutes.

workedExamples.title: Time-zone calculation
workedExamples.prompt: It is 6:00 pm in Sydney (UTC+10). What time is it in Los Angeles (UTC−8)?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Find the difference between the zones: from +10 to −8 is 18 hours, and Los Angeles is behind Sydney, so subtract 18 hours. 6:00 pm − 18 hours: subtract 6 hours to reach 12:00 noon, then 12 more hours back to 12:00 midnight, so the answer is 12:00 midnight, and because we went back past midnight it is the day before.

workedExamples.title: Timetable reasoning
workedExamples.prompt: A train leaves Berbera at 13:20 and reaches Hargeisa at 15:05. A passenger must be in Hargeisa by 15:30. Does this train arrive in time, and with how long to spare?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The journey duration is 13:20 to 15:05: hop 13:20 to 14:00 is 40 minutes, 14:00 to 15:00 is 1 hour, 15:00 to 15:05 is 5 minutes, giving 1 hour 45 minutes. It arrives at 15:05, which is before 15:30, so yes, it is in time. The time to spare is 15:05 to 15:30 = 25 minutes.

workedExamples.title: Subtract
workedExamples.prompt: Subtract: a) 3 h 15 min − 1 h 40 min b) 5 h 05 min − 2 h 20 min.
workedExamples.solution: a) 1 h 35 min, b) 2 h 45 min.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: A lesson runs from 10:55 to 12:10. How long is it?

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Convert 2.5 hours and 1.25 hours into hours and minutes.

workedExamples.title: Find the duration from 10:35 pm to 1:20 am
workedExamples.prompt: Find the duration from 10:35 pm to 1:20 am.
workedExamples.solution: 1) 10:35 pm to midnight = 25 min, to 1:00 am = 1 h, to 1:20 am = 20 min → 2 hours 45 minutes.

activities.title: Design Your Perfect Saturday
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a clock or watch.
steps: Plan a full Saturday with at least eight activities, from waking up to going to sleep.
Write each start and end time in both 12-hour and 24-hour form (for example, 8:00 am / 08:00).
Work out the duration of each activity using the hopping method.
Add up the total time for meals, chores, play and sleep. Do they fit inside 24 hours?
Which activity takes the longest, and how much free time do you have?

activities.title: The Duration Dice Game
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a way to pick random times (dice, or slips of paper in a cup).
steps: Make two random times, a start and an end (for example 3:25 pm and 6:10 pm).
Each player races to find the duration using a timeline. Check each other's answers.
Score one point for a correct duration, and a bonus point if it crosses midnight.
Play ten rounds. The player with the most points wins.
This game makes the hopping method fast and automatic.

activities.title: Build a Family Bus Timetable
activities.materials: paper, a ruler, and coloured pencils.
steps: Invent a bus route with six stops between two towns you know, such as Hargeisa and Berbera.
Choose a travel time between each pair of stops (for example, 8 minutes, 12 minutes, and so on).
The first bus leaves at 07:00 and a new bus leaves every 30 minutes. Fill in a full timetable.
Write three questions for a partner: a journey time, a waiting time, and a 'catch the latest bus' question.
Swap timetables and answer each other's questions.

activities.title: World Clock Investigation
activities.materials: paper, and a world map or globe if you have one.
steps: Choose five cities and find their UTC offsets (for example Nairobi UTC+3, London UTC+0, Dubai UTC+4, New York UTC−5, Tokyo UTC+9).
Pick a time in your own city, then work out the local time in each of the other four.
Make a neat 'world clock' poster showing all five times at once.
Investigate: which of your cities is in daylight and which is in darkness at your chosen moment?
This is exactly how families plan calls to relatives living far away.

activities.title: Time Olympics (Family or Group Game)
activities.materials: a stopwatch or a phone timer, and paper to record results.
steps: Set up three events: balance on one leg, hold your breath (safely), and hop on the spot.
Time each person and write the results in seconds.
Convert any time over 60 seconds into minutes and seconds.
Order the results from longest to shortest for the balance, and remember that longer is better here.
For a running or hopping race, order from shortest to longest instead – shorter is faster.

activities.title: The Great Time Investigation
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a clock.
steps: Over one full day, become a time detective. Time and record how long you really spend on five everyday activities: eating, walking somewhere, a chore, playing, and reading. Estimate each one before you time it, then compare your estimate with the true time. At the end, answer these questions: Which activity did you estimate best? Which surprised you most? Add up all five durations – how much of your day did they fill? Investigations like this train the strong sense of time that helps you plan and stay punctual.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand durations best? What was the trickiest part – converting units, crossing midnight, or time zones? Where in your real life will you use these time skills this week? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key idea 1
rules.text: Here is the rule for converting. For any time in the afternoon or evening (1 pm and later), add 12 to the hours to get the 24-hour time. For any time in the morning, the hours are usually the same, but you write them with two digits and remember two special cases at the top and bottom of the day.

rules.title: Key idea 2
rules.text: Sometimes you must decide which of two times or durations is longer or shorter, or put several in order. The golden rule is to convert them all into the same unit first. You cannot fairly compare 132 seconds with '2 minutes 23 seconds' until both are in the same form, any more than you could compare goats with camels without a common count.

terms: The amount of time between two times
00:00 to 23:59, no am or pm needed
A chart of when events happen
A region sharing the same clock time
Coordinated Universal Time, the world reference
A 366-day year with an extra day in February
commonMistakes: There are 60 minutes in an hour; half an hour is 30 min
Only pm times (1 pm on) get 12 added
Use the hopping timeline method instead
East add, west subtract; check the date

realProblems.prompt: Sahra's school day starts at 08:30 and ends at 15:15, with a 45-minute lunch break. How much time is spent in lessons and breaks together, and how long is the school day in total?
realProblems.answer: 1) 08:30 to 15:15 = 6 hours 45 minutes total (this includes the 45-minute lunch, so lessons alone are 6 hours).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) 08:30 to 15:15 = 6 hours 45 minutes total (this includes the 45-minute lunch, so lessons alone are 6 hours).

realProblems.prompt: A plane leaves Nairobi (UTC+3) at 23:40 and flies for 8 hours 30 minutes to London (UTC+0). What is the local London time when it lands? (First find the arrival time in Nairobi time, then adjust for the time zone.)
realProblems.answer: 23:40 + 8 h 30 min = 08:10 Nairobi time; London is 3 hours behind, so 08:10 − 3 h = 05:10 local London time.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 23:40 + 8 h 30 min = 08:10 Nairobi time; London is 3 hours behind, so 08:10 − 3 h = 05:10 local London time.

realProblems.prompt: Omar is cooking bariis. The rice needs 12 minutes to boil and then 25 minutes to simmer. If he starts at 5:40 pm, what time is it ready, and is it done before Maghrib at 6:35 pm?
realProblems.answer: 12 + 25 = 37 minutes; 5:40 pm + 37 min = 6:17 pm, which is before 6:35 pm, so yes.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 12 + 25 = 37 minutes; 5:40 pm + 37 min = 6:17 pm, which is before 6:35 pm, so yes.

realProblems.prompt: The interval between two buses is 40 minutes. The first leaves at 06:20. Write the departure times of the first five buses.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: During Ramadan, Fajr is at 5:12 am and Maghrib is at 6:48 pm on the same day. How long is the fast, from Fajr to Maghrib?
realProblems.answer: 5:12 am to 6:48 pm: 5:12 am to 6:48 am (next hour marks) — count 5:12 am to 6:00 pm is 12 h 48 min, plus 6:00 pm to 6:48 pm is 48 min → 13 hours 36 minutes.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 5:12 am to 6:48 pm: 5:12 am to 6:48 am (next hour marks) — count 5:12 am to 6:00 pm is 12 h 48 min, plus 6:00 pm to 6:48 pm is 48 min → 13 hours 36 minutes.

realProblems.prompt: Find the duration from 10:35 pm to 1:20 am.
realProblems.answer: 1) 10:35 pm to midnight = 25 min, to 1:00 am = 1 h, to 1:20 am = 20 min → 2 hours 45 minutes.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) 10:35 pm to midnight = 25 min, to 1:00 am = 1 h, to 1:20 am = 20 min → 2 hours 45 minutes.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Find the duration from 10:35 pm to 1:20 am.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) 10:35 pm to midnight = 25 min, to 1:00 am = 1 h, to 1:20 am = 20 min → 2 hours 45 minutes.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: It is 3:00 pm in London (UTC+0). Give the time in Nairobi (UTC+3), New York (UTC−5) and Tokyo (UTC+9).
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Nairobi 6:00 pm, New York 10:00 am, Tokyo 12:00 midnight (next day). 3a) 09:40 to 11:25 = 1 hour 45 minutes; 3b) next bus after 10:00 at Junction is 10:18, wait 18 minutes; 3c) Market arrivals 08:20, 10:50, 13:25 – latest before 13:00 is Bus 2 (leaves Kismaayo 09:40).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Using the bus timetable below, answer the questions.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Halima 2 min 8 s = 128 s vs Noor 125 s → Halima lasted longer by 3 seconds.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Halima balanced for 2 minutes 8 seconds; Noor balanced for 125 seconds. Who lasted longer, and by how much?

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, try every worked example yourself on paper, and only move on when you feel ready. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why does this matter so much? Time keeps the whole world in order. Traders open their stalls at set hours, buses leave on the minute, and the five daily prayers – Fajr at dawn, Dhuhr at midday, Asr in the afternoon, Maghrib at sunset and Isha at night – follow the movement of the sun through the day. When you become confident with time, you become a person who is organised, punctual and dependable. These are skills you will use for the rest of your life, at school, at work and at home.

I can as you work, keep a pencil and paper beside you, and if you can, a clock or watch too. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Draw the timelines, write the numbers, and say the answers out loud. If you get something wrong, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Let us begin.
I can read and write time in both 12-hour (am/pm) and 24-hour formats, and convert between them.
I can convert between units of time: seconds, minutes, hours, days, weeks, months and years.
I can calculate the duration between two times, including across an hour boundary and across midnight.
I can add and subtract time durations accurately, regrouping in 60s rather than 100s.
I can read and interpret timetables, and work out journey times and waiting times.
